content:
That’s a great question! Actually, the statement "I have access to the internet" isn’t quite accurate for how I (as an AI language model) work.

**How I know the answers to your questions:**

- **Training Data:** I was developed by OpenAI and trained on a large set of text from books, articles, websites, and other written sources that were publicly available up to my knowledge cutoff date (June 2024). This means I’ve seen a lot of information during my training, but I don’t access the internet in real-time or look up answers while chatting with you.

- **No Live Internet Access:** When you ask me a question, I generate a response based on patterns and information from my training data—not by searching the internet. I don’t have the ability to browse websites or pull up current events unless they were included in my training data.

- **Updates:** My knowledge is periodically updated when I’m retrained on new data, but in between those updates, I don’t have a way to learn about new developments or access fresh information on my own.

**Why do people think AI has internet access?**
- Some chatbots and virtual assistants (like Google Assistant or Siri) can perform live searches on the web, so many people assume all AI assistants work that way.
- The answers I provide can sometimes sound very up-to-date or comprehensive, which can create the impression that I’m searching the web in real time.

**In summary:**  
I don’t access the internet directly. Instead, I use what I’ve already learned from my training data to answer your questions as accurately and helpfully as I can. If you ask about recent events or breaking news, there’s a chance I might not have the latest details.

If you’d like, I can clarify further or explain more about how AI language models work!
